# Academic Research Sources

These sources provide the older theory and empirical backing behind the tactical system.

## Captions and subtitles

### Video Captions Benefit Everyone

URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5214590/

Key point:

- More than 100 empirical studies document that captioning video improves comprehension, attention, and memory.

Video strategy implication:

```text
Use edited captions by default when speech matters.
```

But separate captions from overlays:

```text
captions = preserve speech meaning
headlines = frame the promise
labels = clarify contrast
annotations = direct attention
CTA text = trigger action
```

## Text overlays

### Standing Out While Fitting In: Visual Design of Text Overlays in Social Media Communication

URL: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00222429251322773

Key points:

- Important text-overlay variables include image dynamism, overlay size, and overlay centrality.
- Large central overlays combined with dynamic images can reduce engagement because the composition becomes visually unappealing.

Video strategy implication:

```text
Text should reduce cognitive load. Do not use heavy central text over busy/dynamic footage unless testing proves it works.
```

## Emotion and virality

### What Makes Online Content Viral?

URL: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmr.10.0353

Key points:

- Virality is partly driven by physiological arousal.
- High-arousal positive emotions such as awe and high-arousal negative emotions such as anger or anxiety are more viral.
- Low-arousal emotions such as sadness are less viral.
- This holds even after controlling for usefulness, surprise, interestingness, and visibility.

Video strategy implication:

```text
Useful content spreads further when paired with active emotion.
```

Safer long-term stacks:

```text
surprise + utility
relief + clarity
awe + proof
frustration + fix
identity recognition + humour
```

Risky stacks:

```text
anger + no solution
fear + exaggeration
curiosity + weak payoff
controversy + no substance
```

## Storytelling and popularity

### A Good Story Well Told: Storytelling Components That Impact Science Video Popularity on YouTube

URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/communication/articles/10.3389/fcomm.2020.581349/full

Key points:

- Study of 306 popular YouTube science videos.
- Popularity related to dramatic question, insight, moments of change, emotional arousal, and story status.
- Emotional arousal showed a strong association with popularity.

Video strategy implication:

```text
Pacing is not cuts. Pacing is story movement.
```

Use:

```text
problem → tension → test → result → insight
```

## Face presence in user-generated videos

### The impact of face presence in user-generated videos on consumer engagement

URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167811625000096

Key points:

- Face presence is generally associated with higher engagement.
- Moderate face presence around 30–40% of frames generated the strongest response.
- Early face presence was especially effective for lower-follower creators.

Video strategy implication:

```text
Use face as an anchor for trust and emotion, then cut to proof, process, screen, object, or transformation.
```

## Older theory used for theses

The thesis bank also draws from:

- AIDA: attention before interest, desire, action.
- Information foraging: viewers estimate value versus effort using information scent.
- Processing fluency: easier-to-process stimuli feel better and more credible.
- Dual coding / multimedia learning: visual and verbal channels should align, not compete.
- Information-gap theory: curiosity comes from a specific gap between what is known and what is wanted.
- Framing theory: the same content changes behaviour depending on whether it is framed as a mistake, myth, experiment, checklist, warning, or teardown.
- Diffusion theory: content spreads when it is adoptable, observable, compatible, and easy to trial.
- Weak ties: shareable content should cross clusters, not only please insiders.
- Behaviour design: motivation + ability + prompt.
- Social proof: comments, examples, analytics, and before/afters lower uncertainty.
- Peak-end rule: the ending shapes remembered value.
- Mere exposure: repeated named formats build familiarity, but over-repetition creates fatigue.
